data.title: Robert Half Inc.: Still Negative On The Near-Term Performance
data.description:
Investment action

I recommended a sell rating for Robert Half Inc. (NYSE:RHI) when I wrote about it in July, as I expected the share price to remain pressured given near-term uncertainties and the lack of a positive catalyst. Based on my current outlook and analysis, I reiterate my sell rating for RHI because of the poor near-term earnings outlook, which I expect to be challenged by lackluster topline performance and negative operating leverage. I acknowledge the potential for a strong recovery over the next two years, but I don’t think the right time to invest is today given the high expectations embedded into RHI’s valuation multiple.

Review

RHI reported its 3Q24 results recently, and performance was not great. Total revenue fell 6.3% y/y to $1.465 billion (though this is better than consensus estimate of -8.2%) on a reported basis. On a same-day constant currency [SDCC] basis, revenue fell by 7.7% y/y in the quarter. While this was slightly better than the 10.1% decline seen in 2Q24, weakness is still apparent across major segments and verticals. Total contract talent solutions fell by 11.9% and 13.2% SDCC. Within it, finance & accounting [F&A] revenue fell by 9.2% y/y, administrative & customer support [A&CS] fell by 9.2% y/y, and technology revenue fell by 6.1% y/y. Permanent placement talent also fell by 11.9%, indicating no signs of recovery (2Q24 fell by 12.2%). The only bright side of these earnings was that Protiviti revenue grew 6.4% y/y, recovering from five quarters of negative y/y growth. Consequently, profit performance did not come in pretty. EBIT margin compressed by 210bps y/y to 6.2% due to negative operating leverage and led to an adj EPS decline of 29.2% to $0.64.

Like I said in my previous update, there is still room for revenue to see y/y decline if we benchmark against the dot-com crisis (RHI saw 10 straight quarters of y/y decline). With the 3Q24 decline, this marked the 8th straight quarter of y/y decline. RHI’s 3Q24 results should put an end to the share price rally and dismiss any potential for near-term recovery. Despite the Fed cutting rates and rather positive economic outlook, these are not translating to strong hiring trends. In fact, the same trend persists, in that businesses continue to delay hiring decisions, and this directly translated to double-digit y/y declines in staffing revenue. Also, similar to last quarter, management pointed out that weakness in hiring sustained into 4Q24, where contract talent solution revenue declined by 12% in the first two weeks of October (this is the same level as the 13% decline seen in 3Q24). Permanent placement talent solution revenue performance is even worse; it has decelerated to a 19% y/y decline in the first three weeks of October vs. the 13% decline seen in 3Q24. I expect this weak trend to continue for the near term as businesses continue to stay wary of the economy (as seen from the US business confidence index), especially with the upcoming uncertain US election.

On a headline basis, 4Q24 is going to look poor as well, given that the quarter has 2.5 fewer days and the winding down of several large projects in Protiviti. This should further weaken the stock’s sentiment for the rest of the year.

The knock-on implication here is the sizeable impact on adj EPS because of the negative operating leverage (we already saw this happen in 3Q24). Management continues to hold on to recruiting capacity, which leads to sizeable decremental margins in a downturn. As a reference, management guidance is implicitly calling for a 280 bps y/y decline at the midpoint in 4Q24.

The one positive aspect that I will point out that may signal a very early sign of recovery is that weekly sequential staffing levels have remained stable over the past 12–14 weeks. According to management, this is the longest consecutive period of stability over the last 24 months, which may suggest a bottom.

Well, so given that all we do is a white collar, white collar is everything. As we said that, we now have the longest period of stability or flatness, we have 14 consecutive weeks of flatness, and we haven't seen that kind of duration over the last two years, which was the period we've been otherwise drifting down sequentially. So the trend, the white collar trend is the overall trend that we referenced, and it's a better trend than we had been seeing. You have to start somewhere. We flattened out. It's more than a week or two. It's more than four or five or six weeks. It's a long time, it's 14 straight weeks. So we're encouraged that we've flattened out for that period of time.

And given the Optimism Index improvements we see from NFIB, given the hope that we get through the election uncertainty relatively quickly, we're optimistic. 3Q24 call

Valuation

Ultimately, the question to answer is when will economic activity pick up strongly, which will lead to an increase in hiring. While I am negative on the near-term, I would say that as the Fed continues to cut rates, the uncertainty impact from the US election goes away, and US GDP continues to grow, we should see a recovery sometime next year in FY26. That will be the right timing to long RHI, as earnings growth will be significant on the back of strong volume/revenue growth and high operating leverage.

I don’t recommend investing today because the near-term earnings outlook is still poor, and the quarter-to-date data suggests that we are in for another poor 4Q24. With the stock still trading at ~21x forward PE (~4x above its through-cycle PE), which implies the market is still hopeful for a near-term recovery, I think the expectations are high. If 4Q24 performs weaker than expected and management guidance/comments for FY25 remain poor (this really depends on the macro situation, which is tough to predict), expectations could reset downwards, leading to a decline in multiples.

Final thoughts

My recommendation remains a sell rating for RHI. The 3Q24 results highlight the continued challenges facing the company in the near term. Weak economic conditions, coupled with the ongoing uncertainty surrounding the US election, should continue to pressure revenue and profitability performance. While there are signs of stabilization in certain areas, I believe the outlook remains weak.

data.title: Very Good News For Big Dividend Infrastructure Stocks
data.description:
Big dividend infrastructure stocks, like the ones in the Cohen&Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF), have boomed recently, as the chart below illustrates. Companies like Williams Companies (WMB), TC Energy (TRP), Enbridge (ENB), Kinder Morgan (KMI), ONEOK (OKE), DT Midstream (DTM), and Targa Resources (TRGP) have surged in recent months.

In this article, I will discuss why the sector has boomed and some of my favorite places to invest in it moving forward.

The Good News For Big Dividend Infrastructure Stocks

Two primary factors are driving the infrastructure stock boom:

First, infrastructure stocks are often considered utility-like in nature and therefore serve as bond proxies. The majority of their cash flows come from assets that are either regulated or under long-term contracts, ensuring stable cash flows over time. This makes them comparable to bonds or utilities. Recently, long-term interest rates have declined due to expectations that the Federal Reserve would cut rates, which has now occurred. As interest rates drop, the net present value of the cash flows generated by these highly contracted and regulated assets increases, driving stock prices higher.

The second, more unique reason for the surge in midstream infrastructure stocks is the boom in artificial intelligence ("AI"). While leading AI stocks like Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Palantir (PLTR) have soared in recent years due to growing demand for AI applications, the focus has recently shifted to the AI infrastructure space. The market now realizes that building the data centers needed to support AI-driven economic growth will require substantial energy.

Although AI companies like Microsoft and Amazon (AMZN) are making efforts to power data centers with renewable energy — such as Microsoft’s recent deal with Brookfield (BAM)(BN)(BEP)(BEPC) and investments in nuclear power with Constellation Energy (CEG) and Vistra (VST) — natural gas is emerging as an essential energy source for these data centers.

Natural gas offers several advantages. First, it provides a more reliable power supply than renewables, as it is not dependent on the sun shining or wind blowing. While battery storage can supplement renewables by providing a more consistent source of power for data centers, it adds extra cost and complexity to the process. In contrast, natural gas power plants offer a straightforward power solution for data centers. Additionally, natural gas power can be quickly ramped up to meet sudden spikes in electricity demand, which is essential for managing the complex workloads of AI models. In contrast, this level of flexibility is more challenging to achieve with renewable energy and even with nuclear power. Natural gas is also relatively inexpensive and cleaner than other fossil fuels like oil and coal, making it an attractive choice for companies that want to balance reliability and flexibility with environmental considerations. As a result, it is expected that natural gas demand will grow considerably in the years to come.

This anticipated growth in natural gas demand has been a significant boost for midstream infrastructure companies, many of which have extensive natural gas infrastructure across North America. Furthermore, numerous existing and planned data centers are located near this infrastructure, making these companies well-positioned for growth in the coming years while also giving them a moat of sorts.

Investor Takeaway

So, what are the best ways to invest in midstream infrastructure right now? Although many midstream infrastructure stocks have surged in recent months, some are still trading at near fair value, making them solid portfolio diversifiers — especially for income-focused investors. Among the options available, Enbridge (ENB) and TC Energy (TRP) stand out as both companies offer high yields, solid growth prospects, and well-diversified business models, making them attractive long-term investments.

However, some of the best opportunities may lie within the master limited partnership (AMLP) part of the midstream sector. While MLPs can deter some investors — particularly institutional funds that prefer C corporations — their unique structure also creates opportunities for those willing to deal with their K1s.

I particularly like Energy Transfer (ET), which has significant natural gas and NGL assets, and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), which offers strong diversification across the midstream sector. Western Midstream (WES) is another solid option because - though its assets are not located near current or planned data centers - it stands to benefit from the broader increase in natural gas demand.

Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) is another compelling infrastructure opportunity right now. It is involved not only in natural gas infrastructure, but also in data center infrastructure, positioning it to benefit significantly from the AI infrastructure boom.

Moreover, as demand for natural gas rises, it will likely have a ripple effect across the broader energy sector as growing natural gas prices (due to strong data center demand) may make oil more attractive for certain applications currently powered by gas, further driving demand for oil. As the saying goes, a rising tide lifts all boats, and I believe now is an excellent time to invest in midstream infrastructure and energy (XLE) in general, given that in many cases energy stock prices have lagged the broader market.

data.title: SLQD: Short Duration Bond Fund From iShares
data.description:
Thesis

iShares is very good at covering all the large building blocks in the financial markets. The ETF provider has staple names in the equity and fixed income markets, names which have enormous liquidity and are traded as proxies for the actual underlying holdings by institutional investors. On the investment grade fixed income side, the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD) is the best-known product, with a $27 billion AUM and ample daily liquidity. LQD addresses the need to access the IG bond universe via an ETF and represents a take on an average IG bond portfolio, including its high duration of 8.5 years.

In order to address the shorter end of the IG bond curve, iShares created the iShares 0-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (NASDAQ:SLQD) with the nomenclature reminding us of 'short-dated' LQD.

In this article, we are going to take a look at the ETF, its features and characteristics and opine on why the name is a buy in today's macro environment.

Fund composition - short-dated IG bonds

As per its own definition, SLQD purchases shorter-dated IG bonds:

The iShares 0-5 Year Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of U.S. dollar-denominated, investment-grade corporate bonds with remaining maturities of less than five years.

The ETF comes with an effective duration of 2 years and a granular portfolio:

The portfolio weighted average maturity is 2.24 years and the effective duration is 2 years. The ETF holds over 2600 names and has a low option-adjusted spread of 50 bps.

The fund holdings fall mostly in the A and BBB rating bands:

We can notice from the above table that A-rated names represent 47% of the holdings, while BBB rated bonds are 43% of the portfolio. Given the low fund duration, the credit risk in the portfolio is very limited, absent another great financial crisis. The portfolio is concentrated in the banking sector, which represents 33% of the fund, followed by consumer non-cyclical at 13%.

SLQD is more of an institutional product

Given its composition and holdings, SLQD is a name that will capture the short end of the IG bond curve and thus will not provide eye-watering results:

Expect a 2% total return in a low rate environment, while today's high risk-free rates macrocycle should command a total return in the 5% to 6% range. To that end, retail investors might not buy into this fund as much since returns are very low. Institutional investors, on the other hand, have required allocation amounts for certain sectors and thus might be enticed by the fund's daily liquidity and high $2 billion AUM.

As an investor here one needs to understand the ETF does not take much in terms of credit risk, this is an instrument mostly driven by front-end rates. The fund comes with low historic returns but also very low volatility (the 3-year standard deviation is 3.3%).

Risk driver - rates

The ETF's main risk driver is represented by front-end rates. The fund has a 4.4% 30-day SEC yield, which is composed of risk-free rates and a credit spread. Given that the 2-year treasury CMT yield is roughly 4%, and the portfolio OAS is 50 bps, we can see how the 30-day SEC yield is composed.

If we do a bit of math on the portfolio yield and its components as described above, we can see that risk-free rates represent roughly 90% of the SEC yield. This means that the prevailing risk driver for this ETF is represented by rates. While there is a small credit component, even if credit spreads blow out and triple from the current levels (i.e., a 100 bps move), we can expect only a -2% down move in the fund assuming the CS01 duration is fairly similar to the rates sensitivity.

We are in an environment of very elevated risk-free rates, and the Fed has engaged in a monetary easing cycle which is set to continue despite inflationary pressures. The 2-year rates are tightly correlated with Fed Funds, so one should fully expect yields to move lower from here. SLQD is not a very exciting or volatile name, but is nonetheless set to benefit from lower rates at the front end of the curve.

Base case scenario and risk factors

In a soft landing scenario where credit spreads remain contained SLQD will gain as Fed Funds and 2-year rates move lower. For 100 bps in lower rates, the ETF will see a 2% capital gain plus its dividend yield. In our base case, the ETF will post a return of roughly 6% in the next 12 months.

The main risk factor for the ETF is represented by the re-emergence of inflationary pressures. A high inflation would force the Fed to pause its easing policy or even hike rates, thus putting pressure on the fund NAV. An inflationary recession scenario (i.e., a recession driven by higher rates) would see credit spreads move higher as well, thus potentially moving the name to a -4% drawdown.

Conclusion

SLQD is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name comes from iShares and is a complementary product to the much better-known LQD fund. SLQD aims to follow the short end of the IG bond curve with a 2-year duration and a granular build. The name has a very low volatility profile but also muted historical total returns. We view SLQD as more of an institutional product via its liquidity and niche. The fund comes with a 50 bps OAS on top of 2-year rates and is slated to make roughly 6% in the next twelve months in a soft landing base case scenario. Rates represent the main risk factor for this name, and from a macro standpoint, we are due to move lower here as the Fed cuts.

data.title: AOD: Massive Distribution Increase, But This Fund Is Still Covering
data.description:
The abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund (NYSE:AOD) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that enjoys a great deal of popularity among income-focused investors. It also carries a certain amount of disdain from some investors due to problems that it had during the few years following the 2009 recession. However, the fund has changed management since that time and the new manager, abrdn, changed the fund's strategy somewhat and resolved the issues that it previously had. Today, the fund is a pretty good way to get equity exposure and a very high yield.

As regular readers likely know, I generally prefer equity funds to fixed-income funds for investors today. This is mostly because I doubt the narrative that inflation is under control, and suspect that fixed-income securities may not provide much in the way of real returns. I explained this thesis in an article from a few days ago, and since that time new data has come out that also suggests that inflation may be more stubborn than policymakers think. In particular, the growth in the M2 in September came in at $80 billion, following a $102 billion increase in August:

That is a 4.6% annualized increase in a single month, which is above the historical 4.1% average. A recent blog post at Schiff Gold makes the following observation:

"The chart below shows the history of inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with ~2 year lag compared to Money Supply. Given this, it is possible that another bout of inflation is lurking just under the surface considering the massive spikes in 2020 and 2021 and the Fed has already started lowering rates."

Here is the chart mentioned in the quote:

Thus, if history does repeat itself, then we could easily see a resurgence of inflation similar to what happened in the lost decade of the 1970s. For those who cannot remember that decade, inflation surged upward and then retreated three times, with each spike of inflation reaching a higher peak than the previous one. This was only stopped when Federal Reserve Chairman Paul Volcker raised interest rates to nearly 20%. As I, and a few other analysts on Seeking Alpha, have speculated, we may be looking at a repeat of the 1970s stagflation in the coming years. However, this time, the Federal Reserve can't raise interest rates high enough to stop it because of the need for the Federal government to service the national debt and run persistent deficits.

As such, anything that does not offer inflation protection will likely be an unattractive holding going forward. This includes most bonds and other fixed-income securities. Real estate, precious metals, and commodities were the best things to hold during the 1970s, but equities did a lot better than bonds. Thus, a fund such as the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund is almost certainly going to be better positioned than any fixed-income fund that an income-focused investor might otherwise consider purchasing.

The abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund currently has an incredibly attractive 13.41% yield. As we can see here, this is substantially higher than the fund's peers possess:

Fund Name

Morningstar Classification

Current Yield

abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund

Equity-Global Equity

13.41%

Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund (EOD)

Equity-Global Equity

9.48%

Calamos L/S Equity & Dynamic Inc Trust (CPZ)

Equity-Global Equity

10.74%

Clough Global Equity Fund (GLQ)

Equity-Global Equity

10.48%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Income Fund (ETG)

Equity-Global Equity

8.18%

Lazard Global Total Return and Income Fund Inc (LGI)

Equity-Global Equity

10.39%

Perhaps surprisingly, we do see that there are a number of global equity funds with yields exceeding 10%. However, the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund is higher than all of its peers by a significant margin. This could be both beneficial and problematic. The beneficial side comes from the fact that this fund will provide its investors with a substantially higher level of income than they would be able to achieve from investing the same amount of money in any other fund. As income-focused investors usually want to achieve the highest possible income from their portfolios that they can, this is likely to be an attractive proposition. However, at the same time, the fact that this fund's yield is so much higher than its peers could be a sign from the market that it will not be likely to sustain its payout. As such, we should take a very close look at this fund's holdings and position before making a purchase.

As regular readers may remember, we previously discussed the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund in late July of this year. The global markets since that time have been incredibly strong, as various market participants are euphoric about the potential productivity boosts that may come from the development of generative artificial intelligence. We have also seen several central banks in developed countries begin to adopt easier monetary policies, which has provided an additional tailwind for the equity markets. Overall, then, we can probably expect the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund to have delivered a respectable performance since our last discussion.

This is indeed the case, as shares of the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund have appreciated by 4.24% since the previous article was published:

As we can see, though, the fund's shares did underperform both the iShares MSCI World ETF (URTH) and the S&P 500 Index (SP500) over the period. This is something that may turn off some investors, but it might not be a huge deal to others. After all, many income-focused investors are willing to sacrifice a certain amount of capital gains in exchange for a higher yield. This fund may have done well enough for investors who fall into that category to be satisfied.

With closed-end funds, however, it is always a good idea to keep in mind that the investors do a lot better than one may think simply by looking at a price chart. As I stated in my previous article on this fund:

A fund's share price performance alone does not provide an accurate picture of how well investors in the fund have actually done. This is because closed-end funds such as the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund typically pay out most or all of their investment profits to the shareholders in the form of distributions. The basic business model is to keep the fund's portfolio at a relatively stable level and give the investors distributions totaling all of the profits earned from the assets. These distributions will not be reflected in the fund's share price performance, but they obviously represent a real return that is realized by the shareholders.

We should thus include the distributions paid by the fund in any analysis of its performance. When we do that, we get this alternative to the chart provided above:

This chart certainly reflects better on this fund as an investment than the price chart alone. As we can immediately see, the shareholders of this fund realized a total return of 6.62% since our last discussion on it. That was actually better than those investors would have received had they simply parked their money in an S&P 500 Index or MSCI World Index fund. This will, overall, almost certainly boost the appeal of this fund in the minds of any potential investors. After all, everybody likes to beat the market.

As we are all well aware, past performance is no guarantee of future results. As such, we should still take a look at the fund's positioning and financial situation before making an investment in it. After all, only by looking at these factors can we draw a reasonably informed conclusion about where this fund is likely to be in the future. The remainder of this article will focus on this task.

About The Fund

According to the fund's website, the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of dividend income. As I pointed out in the previous article though, the website does not provide much information about how the fund aims to achieve this objective. Fortunately, the fund's annual report is a bit better here. Page 63 of the annual report provides the following strategy description for this fund:

The Fund combines four research-driven investment strategies - growth, value, special dividends, and dividend capture rotation - to maximize the amount of distributed dividend income and to identify companies globally with the potential for dividend increases and capital appreciation. The Fund uses a multi-cap, multi-sector, multi-style approach to invest in the securities of any capitalization level (small, mid, or large) and in any sector of industry.

The Fund invests at least 80% of its net assets plus amounts borrowed for investment purposes in equity securities, primarily common stocks, issued by domestic and foreign companies whose equity securities are readily traded on an established U.S. or foreign securities market and pay dividends. The Board of Trustees may change this 80% policy on not less than 60 days' notice to shareholders. The Fund seeks to provide dividend income without regard to whether the dividends qualify for the reduced federal income tax rates applicable to qualified dividends under the Code. Although it is not the Fund's current intent, the Fund may invest up to 100% of its total assets in the securities of non-U.S. issuers and is not restricted on how much may be invested in the issuers of any single country, provided the Fund limits its investments in countries that are considered emerging markets to no more than 35% of the Fund's total assets at any one time. Under normal circumstances, however, the Fund invests 35-80% of its total assets in the securities of non-U.S. issuers and among the securities of issuers located in approximately 10 to 30 countries. Allocation of the Fund's assets to issuers outside of the U.S. and among countries outside of the U.S. is dependent on the economic outlook of those countries and the dividend yields available in their markets. The Adviser believes that this flexibility will allow it to continuously pursue high current dividend income in countries where the Adviser perceives the best opportunities to exist.

This description makes it apparent that the fund invests primarily in common equity securities, which matches the statements that I made in the introduction to this article. Furthermore, this fund seems to have a very stated focus on those companies that pay dividends. There are a lot of closed-end funds, such as Eaton Vance's equity funds, that make the same claim. However, they then invest an outsized proportion of their assets in very richly valued technology stocks that possess a very low yield or do not pay dividends at all. Thus, it can sometimes be difficult to take claims of favoring dividend-paying securities at face value.

The abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund does appear to be somewhat better at focusing its investment portfolio on dividend-paying equities. We can see this by looking at the largest positions currently held by this fund. Here they are:

We do still see three of the mega-cap technology companies in the list, which also tend to be among the largest holdings in most equity funds. However, the fund's statements from the annual report (quoted earlier) simply state that the stocks in which it invests have to pay a dividend. The statements say nothing about its assets needing to possess a certain yield. All the securities shown above meet this requirement, although some of them do have a pretty low yield.

This table summarizes the dividend yields of each of the ten stocks that comprise the fund's largest holdings today:

Company Name

Current Yield

Apple Inc. (AAPL)

0.42%

Microsoft Corporation (MSFT)

0.78%

Broadcom Inc. (AVGO)

1.18%

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL)

0.48%

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM)

1.11%

The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS)

2.32%

JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM)

2.23%

The Coca-Cola Company (KO)

2.79%

AbbVie Inc. (ABBV)

3.28%

AstraZeneca PLC (AZN)

1.92%

According to the Wall Street Journal, the S&P 500 Index currently has a yield of 1.26%:

The trailing 12-month yield of the iShares MSCI World ETF, which tracks the MSCI World Index, is 1.45% as of the time of writing. Thus, we can see that only five of the ten stocks shown above have yields that beat the broad market indices. This is pretty surprising given the fund's goal of generating a high level of current income for its investors. Of course, it is also pretty difficult to keep up with the market without having exposure to the "Magnificent 7" stocks along with Broadcom and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing in today's market environment. The presence of those stocks could thus be an attempt for the fund's managers to earn at least a small amount of dividend income without falling too far behind the market.

It is worth noting, however, that the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund has less of its assets invested in the richly valued (and low-yielding) technology sector than the market indices. The fact sheet includes the following chart:

The information technology sector currently accounts for 32.19% of the S&P 500 Index and 25.23% of the MSCI World Index. This fund, therefore, has lower exposure to this sector than any of the index funds tracking the broader equity market. We can also see that this fund appears to be better diversified than the S&P 500 Index in general.

Sector

AOD Allocation

S&P 500 Allocation

MSCI World Allocation

Information Technology

20.7%

32.19%

25.23%

Financials

17.1%

13.33%

15.71%

Health Care

11.9%

11.25%

11.47%

Consumer Discretionary

10.2%

9.71%

9.87%

Consumer Staples

8.3%

5.81%

6.35%

Industrials

8.2%

8.52%

10.98%

Utilities

5.9%

2.56%

2.69%

Communications Services

5.2%

8.74%

7.50%

Others

11.0%

7.89%

9.90%

Cash

1.5%

0%

0%

(The "Others" segment for the two indices consists of Energy, Materials, and Real Estate.)

We see that the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund is notably overweight Financials, Consumer Staples, Utilities, and Other when compared to the two indices. This actually makes a lot of sense since these sectors tend to have fairly high dividend yields due to the market not assigning high multiples to their cash flows. The fund's large underweight position to the information sector likewise makes a lot of sense since this is one of the lowest-yielding sectors and this fund claims to have a focus on achieving a high level of dividend income. Any fund that is aiming to maximize its dividend income would do well to overweight high-yielding sectors and underweight low-yielding sectors, so this one is doing exactly what we should expect.

The fund's current positioning might also appeal to those investors who are trying to diversify their portfolios away from the "Magnificent 7" stocks that undoubtedly account for a significant exposure for anyone who owns index funds. Several people have left comments on my and other analysts' articles on this site asking for options to achieve this goal. This fund appears to be one such possibility, although it does still have a bit of exposure to the mega-cap technology companies. However, the math still points to improving diversification where an investor moves some money out of a broad-market index fund and puts it into shares of the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund.

Changes Since Our Last Discussion

There have been a few changes to the fund's largest positions list since the last time that we discussed it. These are that TotalEnergies SE (TTE), ASML Holding N.V. (ASML), BE Semiconductor Industries N.V. (OTC:BESIY), and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) were removed from the fund's largest positions. In their place, we have Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan Chase, AbbVie, and AstraZeneca. It is difficult to complain about all of these changes, and personally, I like the removal of both ASML Holding and BE Semiconductor in place of the two banks. This reduced the fund's exposure to the technology sector and improved its diversification overall.

The removal of TotalEnergies removed all energy companies from the fund's largest positions list, but it probably does still have some energy sector exposure. The fund retains a fairly large position in the "Other" sector, which undoubtedly includes energy. However, it is difficult to tell exactly what energy companies may be in the fund today. The most recent holdings report, which is dated July 31, 2024, only lists TotalEnergies and The Williams Companies, Inc. (WMB) among the fund's holdings. That report is three months out of date right now, so it is quite possible that the fund has greater exposure to the high-yielding energy sector than we might think by looking at its sector weightings. After all, anything that it bought in the past three months would not be included in the fund's official filings yet.

With that said, abrdn is a European asset manager and European investors tend to be somewhat more hostile to investing in oil and gas companies than investors in other parts of the world. This fund may be avoiding that sector for this reason. It does still have 11% exposure to companies in a sector not listed though, and it seems likely that this category includes energy, mining companies, and real estate. The fund might have some energy holdings, but it is unclear exactly what companies in that sector it owns today.

Distribution Analysis

The abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of dividend income. It passes its dividend income through to the investors by paying out a monthly distribution of $0.1000 per share ($1.20 per share annually). This gives the fund an impressive 13.41% yield at the current price. As we saw in the introduction, this yield is significantly higher than the fund's peers possess.

The fund, unfortunately, has been inconsistent regarding its distribution over the long term, but it has done pretty well over the past ten years. Here is the fund's distribution history:

In my previous article on this fund, I made the following observations:

We can see a number of distribution cuts over the 2007 to 2013 period. However, since then the fund has been very consistent with respect to its distribution. In early 2014, the fund started paying a monthly distribution of $0.0565 per share and it has not cut since that time. That was more than ten years ago, so we can probably excuse the cuts prior to that time. Abrdn took over the management of this fund in 2018 so everything that the fund did prior to that date is attributable to the previous management, which employed a different strategy.

The fund has been remarkably consistent since abrdn took over its management and the distribution history should overall be pretty attractive to anyone who is seeking to earn a safe and consistent level of income that can be used to pay their bills or finance their lifestyles.

Investors in the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund were almost certainly rewarded by a massive distribution increase that occurred back in August. The fund raised its monthly distribution from $0.575 per share to $0.10 per share:

That is a massive 73.91% increase, which is easily enough to offset the loss of purchasing power that the previous distribution suffered due to inflation over the past three years. Naturally, though, we do want to ensure that the fund can cover this higher distribution as we do not want to suffer a sudden distribution cut.

The last time that we discussed this fund, the most recent financial report was the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on April 30, 2024. That is still the most recent report available today, so it makes no sense to repeat our analysis. As we saw previously, the fund did manage to cover its distributions during that six-month period.

It appears that this fund is still covering its distribution today. This chart shows the fund's net asset value since April 30, 2024 (the closing date of the semi-annual report):

As we can see, the fund's net asset value has been up since that date. This tells us that the investment portfolio's returns were greater than the distributions paid out since that time. This is a good sign, and it strongly suggests that the fund is not distributing too much money and destroying its net asset value. Investors thus should not need to lose any sleep here.

Valuation

Shares of the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund are currently trading at a 10.85% discount to net asset value. This is better than the 9.90% discount that the shares have had on average over the past year. Today's price, therefore, looks quite reasonable.

Expenses

The abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund has an expense ratio of 1.34%, which is quite reasonable when compared to its peers. We can see that here:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Expense Ratio ex-Leverage Carrying Costs

abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund

1.34%

1.14%

Allspring Global Dividend Opportunity Fund

2.81%

1.24%

Calamos Long/Short Equity & Dynamic Income Trust

4.92%

2.00%

Clough Global Equity Fund

3.21%

1.78%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Global Dividend Income Fund

2.63%

1.13%

Lazard Global Total Return and Income Fund

2.22%

1.54%

(All figures from CEF Data.)

We can see that this fund is cheaper than nearly all of its peers. The expense ratio may seem expensive compared to an index fund, but it is quite reasonable compared to other actively managed peers.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the abrdn Total Dynamic Dividend Fund continues to look like a very good closed-end fund for anyone seeking equity exposure and a very high yield. Perhaps surprisingly, the fund appears to be able to sustain its yield, as the current distribution is not destroying the fund's net asset value despite the massive increase a few months ago. In addition, this fund actually looks better diversified than the broad-market indices, which is very appealing to anyone who wants to avoid having too much exposure to the richly valued information technology sector.

Overall, this fund appears to merit consideration.

data.title: T-Mobile, As The Largest Carrier, Faces Major Threats
data.description:
T-Mobile US, Inc. (NASDAQ:TMUS) is the largest U.S. cellular carrier by market capitalization, with a market cap of more than $250 billion. The company has continued to capture market share from other carriers, with a strong 5G network, and grow its FCF as it's cut capex. However, as we'll see throughout this article, the company faces risks that make it a hold at its valuation.

T-Mobile US Customer Growth

T-Mobile has continued to see strong growth within its customer base.

The company saw 315 thousand postpaid net account additions with net customer additions were 1.6 million. This shows how the company has continued to dominate, with 865 thousand postpaid phone net customer additions. The company has also continued to see record churn, and it has continued to build out 5G internet.

The 5G internet based on its existing assets is strong, but we expect 5G internet to lose to hardwired fiber, which will continue to beat it in both speed and latency. It's the same logic that SpaceX (SPACE) is great for rural users but will never be truly competitive in a city due to latency.

T-Mobile US Financial Performance

Financially, the company's growth has continued to support its overall financial strength.

The company saw 5% YoY revenue growth with incredibly strong EPS growth and net cash growth. The company saw more than $5 billion in FCF in the quarter, although its 1.6% dividend remains one of the weakest in the industry. The company does have a lofty share buyback program, and we would like to see other wireless companies ramp up their buybacks.

The company's shareholder returns of $5.6 billion annualized, partially due to the company's lofty market cap, are a 2% annualized return rate. It's also worth noting that the company's FCF is driven by the lowest net debt of the major cellular carriers, however, with an almost $80 billion net debt load, it doesn't have substantial room to expand that.

The company's guidance is for ~$16.85 billion in adjusted FCF for 2024, supported by a decline in capex to $8.9 billion. That's the lowest capex of the major cellular providers. All this puts the company at a ~7% FCF yield, which is the lowest of the major cellular companies. That shows how the company has a loftier valuation that requires growth.

Given the companies much lower capex partially driving that FCF, that's a risk worth paying close attention to.

T-Mobile US 5G Advantage

The company continues to maintain the advantage of its incredibly strong 5G portfolio.

This comes after a major 2021 auction, where Verizon was forced to spend almost $50 billion on spectrum to catch up. T-Mobile continues to have the highest availability of more than 50% and the company has been able to ramp down capex as a result. AT&T Inc. (T) and Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ) are having to keep expenses high as they build out their 5G networks.

While this shows T-Mobile's strength, and we expect that it's a big driver of those switching to T-Mobile, Verizon, and AT&T clearly realize they're behind, and they're working to catch up. As this advantage declines, we expect T-Mobile's competitive advantage and therefore their growth to slow down as a result.

T-Mobile US Competition Risks

T-Mobile has substantial competition risks as the company focuses primarily on its cellular business.

The above chart from AT&T's earnings report shows the company's growing home fiber internet subscriber count. More importantly, it shows the % of AT&T fiber subscribers with AT&T mobility, which has grown towards 40%. AT&T also maintains HBO Max as a bundle, from its days of ownership of Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. That ability to offer synergies across its assets is new competition.

The company's <$10 billion in annual capex is less than half of peers like AT&T. That competition will potentially slow down T-Mobile's growth and ability to drive shareholder returns over the long run.

Thesis Risk

T-Mobile US has managed to grow dramatically going into 2024 and the company continues to be rewarded for its brilliant acquisition of Sprint along with its acquisition of US Cellular. That's enabled the company's capex to be much lower, which has enabled its higher FCF and shareholder returns. That could justify its valuation and make the company a buy at this point.

Conclusion

T-Mobile has a market capitalization of more than $250 billion, making it the largest cellular carrier in the United States. The company has come a long way, supported by intelligent decisions around 5G, and the company's intelligent acquisitions. As a result, it has the lowest debt and capex of the major telecom providers.

Going forward, T-Mobile US, Inc. trades at a 7% FCF yield, which means that it will need continued growth to justify its valuation. The company's shareholder returns among the big ones are fairly modest. Combined with future risks about not being able to cross-sell customers and the risks of lower capex, and we rate the company as a hold at this time.

data.title: HYLB: A Cheap Fee Doesn't Make This Asset Class More Attractive
data.description:
Strategy

Xtrackers USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:HYLB) started investing operations on 12/07/2016 and tracks the Solactive USD High Yield Corporates Total Market Index. It has a distribution yield of 6.02% and an expense ratio of 0.05%. Distributions are paid monthly. As described by Xtrackers in the prospectus, the underlying index selects US listed, US dollar denominated high yield corporate bonds issued by companies from developed markets. Eligible securities must have:

  • A composite rating calculated as sub-investment grade
  • Issuers with at least $1 billion outstanding face value
  • At least $400 million of outstanding face value
  • An original maturity date at most 15 years
  • At least one year to maturity (20 months for new constituents).

Sub-investment grade bonds are often referred to as “junk bonds.” The portfolio turnover rate in the most recent fiscal year was 26%.

Portfolio

About 84% of asset value is in debt issued by US companies, 4% in Canada, 5% in Europe and 3% in Asia. The two heaviest sectors in the portfolio are consumer discretionary (16.8% of asset value) and communication services (14.6%). Energy, industrials and financials weigh between 10% and 12%, while other sectors are below 9%.

The portfolio has 1107 constituents. The next table lists the top 10 holdings, which represent only 3.7% of asset value.

Performance

The next chart compares total returns since inception of HYLB and two close competitors tracking total market high-yield bond indexes:

  • iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG)
  • SPDR Bloomberg High Yield Bond ETF (JNK)

They both have a higher expense ratio (0.49% and 0.40%, respectively).

HYLB is marginally ahead of its peers. However, the share price has lost almost 10% since inception, as plotted on the next chart. While it has been paying high distributions, the fund has suffered a loss in capital. In the same time, the cumulative inflation was about 30%, based on CPI. For shareholders, it represents a loss of 37% in inflation-adjusted value in less than 7 years.

Additionally, the annual sum of distributions has decreased by about 12% between 2017 and 2023, from $2.36 to $2.08 per share. The decay in dividends is also amplified by inflation: about 24% in the same time. For shareholders, it represents a loss of 33% in inflation-adjusted income in 6 years.

HYLB vs. strategy ETFs

The next table compares characteristics of HYLB and four high-yield corporate bond ETFs aiming to improve risk-adjusted performance with rules-based methodologies:

  • iShares 0-5 Year High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (SHYG)
  • VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (ANGL)
  • First Trust Tactical High Yield ETF (HYLS)
  • FlexShares High Yield Value-Scored Bond Index Fund (HYGV).

The next chart plots total returns, starting on 7/18/2018 to match all inception dates. HYLB is second to last, beating only HYLS. The “fallen angels” ETF, focused on debt of companies having lost their investment grade, is the best performer.

Takeaway

Xtrackers USD High Yield Corporate Bond ETF is a broad market ETF in high-yield bonds. HYLB is quite diversified across sectors, with a focus on consumer discretionary and communication. Since its inception, HYLB has marginally outperformed its competitors HYG and JNK. However, historical data point to a decay of over 30% in inflation-adjusted value and distributions in 7 years.

Despite a high-yield and low fee, but HYLB isn't really compelling as a long-term investment. Nonetheless, it may be profitable in swing trading and tactical allocation strategies. Investors seeking exposure to high-yield corporate bonds for a long period may prefer fallen angels ETFs like VanEck Fallen Angel High Yield Bond ETF (ANGL) and iShares Fallen Angels USD Bond ETF (FALN).

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Plummets
data.description:
Bullish sentiment among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks decreased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, both neutral sentiment and pessimism increased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 7.7 percentage points to 37.7%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the 50th time in 51 weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, increased 3.2 percentage points to 32.4%. Neutral sentiment is above its historical average of 31.5% for the first time in 16 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 4.5 percentage points to 29.9%. Bearish sentiment is below its historical average of 31.0% for the 10th time in 11 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 12.3 percentage points to 7.8%. The bull-bear spread is above its historical average of 6.5% for the 24th time in 25 weeks.

This week's special question asked AAII members what information they are particularly paying attention to as companies are reporting earnings.

Here is how they responded:

  • Whether earnings were better or worse than expected: 29.1%.
  • Guidance on future revenues and earnings: 26.5%.
  • Sales and/or earnings growth: 22.8%.
  • Fundamentals such as the balance sheet or cash flow: 16.4%.
  • Other: 5.3%.

This week's Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 37.7%, down 7.7 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 32.4%, up 3.2 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 29.9%, up 4.5 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: APA: Raised Callon Synergies, Margin-Accretive Divestment And Suriname FID Confirm Our Rating
data.description:
We initiated our coverage of APA Corp. (NASDAQ:APA), previously known as Apache, in June with an Overweight rating. In the report, we centered our thesis on 2 key arguments: 1) an improving portfolio with the company's interests in Suriname likely a key midterm driver and 2) a highly discounted valuation due to a relatively higher cost base and concerns about the Callon deal. Since then, shares have dropped significantly, currently being down ~16% as falling crude prices and an overall weakening near term outlook for oil markets have dragged on investor sentiment.

However, we note that APA shares have performed relatively well compared to peers, which we view as acknowledgement of 1) an already heavily discounted valuation, 2) solid operational progress on acquired Callon assets and 3) a ~10% unit cost accretive non-core divestment. We especially note that both companies performing better than APA, that being Permian Resources and Magnolia, have a significantly lower cost portfolio which should bode well for outperformance in a potentially weaker 2025 price environment and therefore view APA's performance as somewhat acknowledging a bottom on multiples.

With the Suriname project also officially FID'ed and expected to deliver the first barrels of ~$30-40/bbl breakeven, high-quality crude by 2028, we believe APA shares continue to provide good value with a credible catalyst to improve valuation over the mid term.

We reiterate our Overweight but reduce our price target by ~11% to $36/sh on a lower oil price deck, yet still implying a sector-high ~46% upside. Key risks remain primarily in a weaker oil macro and company-specific factors such as exploration and/or development failures across the Permian and Suriname.

[Note: US smallcap E&P peers include Permian Resources (PR), Matador (MTDR), Magnolia O&G (MGY), Civitas (CIVI), Marathon Oil (MRO) and Chord Energy (CHRD).]

Key Discussion Points

Callon cost synergies run-rate target revised upwards from $150MM to $250MM. During the Q2 call APA management confirmed it had achieved a sizeable portion of projected synergies and revised the target up by ~66% to now a run-rate of $250MM. While some of those $100MM in additional synergies are expected to come from overhead spending, as one would expect primarily, the majority of it is to be contributed through greater operational synergies. Raising its op. synergy target from $55 to $120MM, management cites the strong initial progress in aligning performance at acquired wells to legacy APA wells. As noted in our previous note, the great degree of performance delta between APA's and Callon's wells had been a key concern regarding the acquisition. We also note that total synergies are now valued at ~$2.5B or roughly 55% of total deal value, which screens very favorable vs other recent large cap, US-onshore deals.

$950MM divestment of Permian non-core Central Basin Platform to decrease domestic LOE/boe by ~10%. In order to pay down the sizeable debt incurred through the acquisition of Callon, management aims at targeted divestments of lower-return areas to streamline and optimize the portfolio. While we would have most likely expected such divestments in the North Sea, where APA struggles with relatively higher costs, the company recently announced it would sell the entirety of its Central Basin Plateau assets. Realizing gross proceeds of $950MM, we believe the divestment should improve APA's position in the Permian, centering its operations on higher value acreage in the Delaware and Midland. We also highlight management noting the divested acreage and assets as providing far fewer incremental drilling opportunities; therefore the divestment should not be expected to materially impact future production growth potential in the basin.

Management also noted a direct positive impact of the divestment on company-wide unit production costs, estimating it to yield a ~10% reduction in Lease Operating Expenses ("LOE") on its US domestic assets. While the Callon deal had an immediately dilutive effect on unit LOE, we believe due to this divestment LOEs should improve through the later half of the year and into 2025.

Still, we continue to view APA's asset base as somewhat suboptimal, with especially its North Sea assets lowering company-wide cost competitiveness while Egypt assets remain exposed to political risk. Therefore, further divestments of non-core assets are likely needed in the midterm in order to fully optimize APA's footprint on the two pillars of the Permian and Suriname.

Suriname officially FID'ed with the initial 220Kboed FPSO delivering first oil by 2028. On October 1st, APA and its JV partner TotalEnergies (TTE) officially sanctioned the first development in the highly prospective Block 58 offshore Suriname. We have covered the project, including, in our view, its very advantaged economics in more detail in our most recent report on Total (see here), but in short we continue to see the development as likely being the key driver to APA's valuation in the midterm and its key differentiator vs. fellow US small-cap E&Ps.

Valuation Update

We keep our valuation methodology unchanged but incorporate a slightly lower forward oil price deck ($84/82 Brent for 24/25E), leading to a 11% decline in our price target to $36/sh, yet still implying the most significant upside potential among our US E&Ps coverage.

Lower prices are partially offset by an increase in Suriname's estimated reserves from 700 to 800Mboe, raising our est. value of the asset by ~11% to around $5.8/sh (~16% of price target) which we believe accurately captures the company's midterm outlook.

data.title: EUR/USD Update - Technicals Hint At Bounce But Acceptance Above 1.08 Is Key
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

The Euro has lost some of its early Thursday gains as US PMI data exceeded expectations. A weaker US Dollar has helped the euro, while the technicals have also been flashing signs of a potential bounce for the pair.

PMI Insights: A Story of Diverging Outcomes

Looking at the data today, Euro PMI data continued its decline. The HCOB Flash Eurozone Composite PMI increased slightly to 49.7 in October, up from a seven-month low of 49.6 in September, yet it fell just short of the predicted 49.8 and indicated a slight contraction in business activity. Growth in the services sector slowed a bit (51.2 compared to 51.4), while the decline in manufacturing eased (45.9 versus 45).

Companies scaled back production due to weakening demand, with new orders decreasing for the fifth consecutive month. As a result, firms reduced employment at the most significant rate in nearly four years, and business confidence hit an 11-month low. Meanwhile, input costs rose at their slowest rate since November 2020, and output charge inflation dropped to a 44-month low.

Germany and France remained the main contributors to the decline, experiencing further significant reductions in business activity.

The US PMI data painted a completely different picture and thus raises concerns about a euro recovery. The S&P Global US Composite PMI increased to 54.3 in October 2024 from 54.0 in September, indicating robust growth in business activity at the beginning of the fourth quarter, according to preliminary data. This growth was primarily fueled by the service sector, which recorded a PMI of 55.3, while manufacturing output saw its third consecutive month of contraction with a PMI of 47.8.

Employment experienced a slight decline for the third month in a row, reflecting uncertainty surrounding the upcoming Presidential Election. However, confidence looking forward improved after a steep decline in September, as businesses anticipated greater stability following the election. The survey also revealed a slowdown in inflation for both input costs and prices charged, with a notable decrease in inflation within the service sector.

The diverging paths of the two economies look set to extend into monetary policy as well. At Least this seems to be the biggest risk to a euro recovery as markets price in more aggressive cuts from the ECB than the US Federal Reserve. This could affect EUR/USD in both the short and longer term.

Tomorrow we have a bunch of medium tier data releases from both the US and the EU which should not have a massive impact on EUR/USD. A bunch of speakers from both the ECB and Fed are also on the docket and that see adjustments to rate cut expectations.

.

Technical Analysis

EUR/USD has been on a move lower since forming a double top pattern at the key 1.1200 handle. The move has been swift, and this leaves the door open for a potential retracement.

There is consensus beginning to build for a potential bullish move. The RSI on the daily has printed a higher low with price having made a lower low, this divergence in play. The RSI is also eyeing a cross back above 30 levels hinting at shifting momentum.

Looking at the candlesticks and we could have a bullish engulfing daily close, which would be excellent for potential longs. This would just add another form of confirmation to the potential setup.

This is a very technical setup, as I pointed out above the fundamental picture really points towards continued US Dollar dominance and weakness for the Euro. The technicals however are starting to paint an intriguing picture.

EUR/USD Daily Chart, October 24, 2024

Support

  • 1.0755
  • 1.0700
  • 1.0600

Resistance

  • 1.0800
  • 1.0840
  • 1.0900

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Root Inc. Q3 Earnings Preview: Loss Ratio Not Sustainable
data.description:
I am updating my previous analysis on Root, Inc. (NASDAQ:ROOT) in advance of Q3 earnings, which will be released post-market on October 30th.

I previously rated Root a sell for the following reasons:

  • The valuation at the time required everything to go right, with no change in loss ratios and 30%+ revenue growth.
  • DCF analysis generated a price target of $11.36, 17% downside from pricing at the time.
  • The company was facing headwinds from slow industry growth, and riskier new customers.

Since then, Root is up an impressive 128% while the S&P 500 (SP500) has risen only 14%.

Root is a high-growth company with a solid, technology-based, business model. Root is also on a roll of overdelivering on earnings consensus, meaning the odds are in investors favor of good news next week.

That said, I believe the market is overvaluing the potential and not considering the impact of scale and competition on the P&L. Supporting the current price requires a combination of growth and margin expansion that my modeling suggests is unreasonable, as Root needs to turn to riskier and riskier customers to succeed.

While there is upside potential if management can use their technology to unlock the middle of the market or new lines of business, the industry constraints are difficult to overcome.

At a price target of $30.57, 21% downside from today, I maintain my sell rating on Root, Inc.

Q3 Earnings Preview

Root is expected to announce EPS of ($1.09) and revenue of $271 million, which are both down sequentially.

EPS consensus revisions are mixed, with 4 up and 2 down. Revenue is on the negative side with 1 up and 5 down.

Except for one quarter, Root has beaten the EPS consensus, most often by double digits. This suggests management guides conservatively, and we are more likely to see at least a hit if not a beat.

While management doesn't provide earnings guidance, they did say they are looking at near-term profitability. Q3 consensus is certainly far from profitability. With that in mind, I will be closely looking at underlying drivers as earnings come out, especially new customers, renewal customers, and loss ratio, to understand what growth may look like near term.

Valuation

I do not typically run a discounted cash flow, or DCF, analysis on a pre-profit company, but Root is nearing profitability barring a black swan event. In addition, I wanted to see what the current pricing requires in the way of growth rates.

First, I am running two scenarios with the following assumptions relevant to both:

  • Long-run growth at 1% based on an industry CAGR of 2.2% and building in margin deterioration assuming the company scales near-term.
  • A discount rate of 10.7% with a 4 percentage point risk premium on top of the estimated WACC due to the company stage and future financing needs.
  • SG&A cost growth rate at 50% of revenue growth rate to adjust for scale.
  • No material change to operating cash structure or CapEx structure.

In the first scenario, I assume that loss rates stay constant, and I scale the revenue growth rate to deliver the current price target. This requires a sustained 28% revenue growth rate at consistent cost of revenue and scaling SG&A.

In the second scenario, I assume that the loss rate deteriorates 2 percentage points each year through 2027. This requires a 65% revenue growth rate to be sustained through 2027 while scaling SG&A.

For my price target case, I am assuming loss rate deterioration, slightly less scale on SG&A and revenue growth starting at the current CAGR and deteriorating annually.

This analysis generates a price target of $30.57.

Loss Ratio Is Not Sustainable

As discussed above, the US auto insurance industry has a long-run growth forecast of 2.2%. This means that at the double-digit growth rates, Root needs (even at much lower growth rates) they have to take market share.

To take market share, you require a more compelling offering. Root's strategy is to use technology to put themselves in front of consumers with low risk, and offer them lower prices. Thus far, that strategy has worked well.

The challenge is twofold. First, there are only so many low-risk customers and favorable markets. At some point, to continue growing aggressively, you have to move into riskier customers and markets. Note that Root has been actively avoiding certain markets with lower profitability.

At some point, to scale, you begin regressing to the industry mean, which is currently in the mid-70s.

The second thing that happens in such a slow-growth market, is as you start taking market share, competitors will push back more and more aggressively to retain those low-risk customers. You cannot model Root's growth in a vacuum, as I fear management (and the market) are doing.

Both of these factors combined reduce either top-line growth potential or gross margin below a level that supports the current share price, as I modeled above.

Upside Potential

Upside potential for Root comes from two factors. The first is using their technology to unlock the middle of the market from a risk standpoint. As you move to the middle of the market, the ability to undercut on price becomes tighter, and the risk of higher loss becomes higher. That said, there is a chance they unlock this ability from their predictive technology and drive sustainable growth at a higher rate than I have modeled.

The second factor is that Root moves into other lines of business and has similar levels of success. This is always challenging from a customer acquisition and name recognition standpoint, but the possibility is there that Root unlocks this to maintain growth rates.

Verdict

Root is certainly disrupting the industry, and in their niche they have found a competitive advantage. The challenge I see is that they need to expand to a broader and broader customer base to hit the growth rates that support the current price target. Widening the customer base risks increasing loss ratios and deteriorating margin, which would again cause the current price target to not be supported.

At a price target of $30.57, 21% downside from today, I maintain my sell rating on Root, Inc.
